chunk_summary: Guidelines for transitioning SAM patients from F-75 to F-100 feeding, emphasizing safety, monitoring for complications, and achieving catch-up growth while preventing refeeding syndrome.
learning_objectives: Understand the criteria for transitioning SAM patients from stabilization to rehabilitation feeding.
Learn the structured approach to replacing F-75 with F-100 and monitoring for cardiac intolerance.
Recognize and manage complications such as refeeding syndrome and poor catch-up growth.
Interpret weight gain metrics and their implications during rehabilitation feeding.
key_facts: Transition to F-100 feeding begins when appetite returns, temperature stabilizes, hypoglycemia resolves, and oedema reduces or resolves.
Replace F-75 with an equal volume of F-100 for 2 days, then gradually increase feed volume until small amounts remain uneaten.
Daily weight gain is categorized as good (≥10 g/kg/day), moderate (5 to <10 g/kg/day), and poor (<5 g/kg/day).
Monitor for signs of cardiac intolerance, including tachypnea, rising pulse, hepatomegaly, or recurrent oedema.
Refeeding syndrome prevention includes phased feeding, early potassium and magnesium repletion, and close monitoring.
Frequent feeding (8 feeds over 24 hours) is recommended during the rehabilitation phase.
reference_quote: Begin rehabilitation only when appetite has returned, temperature is stable, there has been no further hypoglycemia, and oedema is reducing or resolved. (Source: Ministry of Health and Family Welfare, 2011)
document_key_topics: Transition from F-75 to F-100 feeding
Monitoring for cardiac intolerance and refeeding syndrome
Catch-up growth and weight gain metrics
